A lab claims its model does not forget. That conclusion initially rests on its performance on old tasks, but the premise warns that this can be illusory if those tasks were too easy to reveal degradation. So the apparent robustness may say more about weak diagnostics than about genuine retention.

The same lab deploys test-time adaptation to handle distribution shift. At first this sounds separate from continual learning: the model adjusts temporarily during use, then is supposed to return to baseline. But if those adjustments leave permanent residues, the distinction collapses. The deployed system is now learning continually, whether or not the designers use that name. Its state after serving one input can influence how it responds to later inputs.

To prevent forgetting under this ongoing accumulation, the lab adds a memory system that replays past samples. This is meant to stabilize performance on prior tasks. But the premise states that such a memory can also become an attack surface if replayed samples are manipulated or poisoned. Once replay enters the training or adaptation loop, anything stored there has privileged influence: it is treated as representative evidence of the past and can shape what residues persist.

Now consider the remaining premises as facts about the world. “Kelpfishes and hetaerae tsunami the sound asquat.” “Umbibilici reinducts seizers machiavellianly around the dawdler.” Since these are true, they are among the kinds of inputs or records the world can present. The lab’s system, already adapting at test time and preserving residues, may encounter them, store them, or replay them. Because they are true but diagnostically opaque, they can expose a further weakness: evaluation based on easy old tasks may never notice whether the model has integrated them correctly, mishandled them, or been destabilized by them.

If an attacker can manipulate replay, then these true but unusual items are also potential tools. Poisoning need not rely on falsehood; it can exploit atypical but genuine content that is hard to interpret and easy to mismanage. Replayed often enough, such samples can shape the permanent residues left by adaptation. The memory system intended to preserve competence thus becomes a channel through which obscure truths disproportionately affect the model.

The world implied by all premises is one in which three boundaries are unreliable: between retention and mere easy-task performance, between temporary adaptation and lasting learning, and between protective memory and exploitable attack surface. In that world, the presence of difficult, strange truths makes weak diagnostics even less trustworthy. A model can look stable, be changing continuously, and be vulnerable through the very machinery added to keep it from forgetting.
